title: Understanding endpapers
subtitle: What endpapers are, why they matter, and how to choose and fold them
excerpt: Endpapers are the leaves that bridge the text block to the boards in a case-bound book. They carry the structural load of opening, hide the raw turn-in edges, and set the first visual impression when the cover is opened. Choosing and preparing them well is one of the decisions that most affects both the durability and the look of a finished book.
sourceNotes: Arthur Johnson, The Thames and Hudson Manual of Bookbinding (1978), chapter on endpapers. Also draws on Esther K. Smith, How to Make Books (2007, Potter Craft), chapter on materials.

glossaryTerms.definition: The endpaper leaf glued flat against the inside face of a bookboard, hiding the raw turn-in edges of the covering material.
The endpaper leaf that is not glued to the board, it folds into the text block as its first or last leaf, opening with the book.

Machine-made paper has a grain direction: the majority of fibres lie parallel to it. Paper folds more crisply along the grain and buckles when moisture is applied against the grain. In endpapers, the grain must run parallel to the spine to prevent warping.

content.text: Every case-bound book has four endpaper leaves: a front
, then a mirror pair at the back. The paste-downs, once glued, take every opening stress the boards will experience. The free endpapers are the reader's first touch of the book's interior.
Most endpaper failures come from wrong

. When you glue a damp paste-down to a board, the moisture causes the paper to swell perpendicular to the grain. If the grain runs head-to-tail (parallel to the spine), the swell pushes the board apart from the spine, this is what causes a correctly-made book to lie flat after pressing. If the grain runs across the spine, the swell pushes the boards away from each other, causing a banana-curve warp that does not relax.

Bend the paper loosely in one direction, then the other: the direction it bends most easily with least resistance is parallel to the grain. Alternatively, lick one edge: the edge curls toward the grain direction as the moisture causes the fibres on that face to swell. Mark the grain direction with a light pencil arrow before cutting.

An endpaper needs to be heavier than the text-block paper, typically 100-140 gsm, because it takes more stress per leaf than any interior page. Plain cartridge at 120 gsm is the reliable all-purpose choice. Decorative papers (marbled, printed, coloured) can be used for the paste-down if they are not too thin; paste them with dilute PVA and press thoroughly to prevent cockling. For a half-Bradel or quarter binding, matching the endpaper colour to the side board paper gives a more finished look when the book is opened.

Each endpaper is a single sheet folded in half: the fold becomes the joint hinge between the text block and the board. Cut the sheet so that when folded, the grain runs parallel to the fold (and therefore to the spine). Fold firmly with a bone folder; do not glue the fold. Wrap the folded sheet around the first (or last) signature of the text block. At casing-in, glue only the outer leaf (the future paste-down), protecting the inner leaf with a slip of waste paper. The inner leaf stays free.

Marbled paper, paste paper, and screen-printed paper all make excellent endpapers; the tutorials in the marbling and journalling sections show how. The key constraint is weight: if the decorated paper is below 90 gsm, reinforce it by folding a plain 80 gsm sheet inside it before attaching to the signature. Very thick decorated papers (above 150 gsm) will stiffen the joint and prevent flat opening.
